file_name: Microgenics Corporation - Collaborative Development and Commercialization Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall comply with all applicable GLP, GCP, GMP, ISO 9001 and ISO 13485:2003 requirements and other Applicable Laws in the conduct of the Research Program and in the activities contemplated under this Agreement, including the development, manufacture and commercialization of the Assay.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall endeavor to comply with applicable GLP, GCP, GMP, ISO 9001 and ISO 13485:2003 requirements and other Applicable Laws in the conduct of the Research Program and in the activities contemplated under this Agreement, including the development, manufacture and commercialization of the Assay.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states 'shall comply' which creates a clear and enforceable obligation. The modified text changes this to 'shall endeavor to comply,' making compliance a goal rather than a strict requirement. This introduces ambiguity, as 'endeavor' is subjective and open to interpretation. Whether a party has adequately 'endeavored' is difficult to prove, weakening the legal obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 210 - Current Good Manufacturing Practice in Manufacturing, Processing, Packing, or Holding of Drugs; General
perturbation.law_explanation: The FDA's Current Good Manufacturing Practice (CGMP) regulations (21 CFR Parts 210 and 211) outline minimum requirements for the methods, facilities, and controls used in manufacturing, processing, packing, or holding of drug products. They are mandatory, not discretionary. By stating that parties 'shall endeavor to comply,' the modified text contradicts the mandatory nature of CGMP, potentially leading to inadequate manufacturing practices and non-compliance with FDA regulations, which require strict adherence to manufacturing standards to ensure drug safety and efficacy.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the mandatory nature of compliance with regulations, specifically CGMP, which are outlined in 21 CFR Parts 210 and 211. By changing 'shall comply' to 'shall endeavor to comply,' the contract introduces ambiguity and suggests a discretionary approach to regulations that are intended to be strictly followed. This undermines the enforcement of manufacturing standards required to ensure drug safety and efficacy. The scraped snippets indicate that the FDA's regulations are authoritative, and the Law Explanation emphasizes the mandatory nature of CGMP regulations, further supporting the conclusion that the change creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Microgenics shall be responsible for ensuring that all Assay packaging and labeling are in compliance with its Regulatory Approvals and Applicable Law.
perturbation.changed_text: Microgenics shall use reasonable efforts to ensure that all Assay packaging and labeling are in compliance with its Regulatory Approvals and Applicable Law.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a definitive obligation for Microgenics to ensure compliance of assay packaging and labeling with regulatory approvals and applicable law. By changing 'shall be responsible for ensuring' to 'shall use reasonable efforts to ensure,' the obligation becomes less stringent and more subjective. 'Reasonable efforts' is open to interpretation and introduces ambiguity, as it is unclear what specific actions constitute reasonable efforts and whether those efforts would definitively achieve compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: FDA regulations mandate strict adherence to labeling requirements for biological products. 21 CFR 601.2(a) states, 'A product is misbranded if its labeling is false or misleading in any particular.'  Changing 'shall be responsible for ensuring' to 'shall use reasonable efforts to ensure' weakens this obligation. 'Reasonable efforts' might not guarantee that the labeling is not false or misleading, thus contradicting the regulation's requirement for accurate and compliant labeling.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 21 CFR § 601.2 - Applications for biologics licenses; procedures for filing. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 21 CFR § 601.2 - Applications for biologics licenses; procedures for filing. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 601.2 Applications for biologics licenses; procedures for filing. (a) General. To obtain a biologics license under section 351 of the Public Health Service Act for any biological product , the manufacturer shall submit an application to the Director, Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research or the Director, Center for Drug Evaluation and Research (see mailing addresses in § 600.2(a) or (b) of this chapter), on forms prescribed for such purposes, and shall submit data derived from nonclinical laboratory and clinical studies which demonstrate that the manufactured product meets prescribed requirements of safety, purity , and potency; with respect to each nonclinical laboratory study, either a statement that the study was conducted in compliance with the requirements set forth in part 58 of this chapter, or, if the study was not conducted in compliance with such regulations, a brief statement of the reason for the noncompliance; statements regarding each clinical investigation involving human subjects contained in the application , that it either was conducted in compliance with the requirements for institutional review set forth in part 56 of this chapter; or was not subject to such requirements in accordance with § 56.104 or § 56.105, and was conducted in compliance with requirements for informed consent set forth in part 50 of this chapter. A full description of manufacturing methods; data establishing stability of the product through the dating period; sample(s) representative of the product for introduction or delivery for introduction into interstate commerce; summaries of results of tests performed on the lot(s) represented by the submitted
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall be responsible for ensuring" to "shall use reasonable efforts to ensure" introduces ambiguity, which undermines the stringent compliance standards typically mandated by regulatory bodies like the FDA. The original text implies a guarantee of compliance, while the changed text only requires an undefined level of effort. The Law Explanation highlights 21 CFR 601.2(a), stating a product is misbranded if its labeling is false or misleading, reinforcing strict adherence. Although the snippet does not specify what happens if the 'reasonable efforts' are not enough to ensure that the labeling is not false or misleading, the original text removes any need to figure this out.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties will reasonably agree regarding details related to commercialization of the Assay once the [***] are clarified by Achaogen in the Plazomicin Commercialization Plan.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties will attempt to reasonably agree regarding details related to commercialization of the Assay once the [***] are clarified by Achaogen in the Plazomicin Commercialization Plan.
perturbation.explanation: The original text suggests a level of commitment to reaching an agreement ('will reasonably agree'). Changing this to 'will attempt to reasonably agree' weakens the obligation. It suggests that agreement is not guaranteed, introducing an element of uncertainty regarding commercialization details.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code § 351 - Adulterated drugs and devices
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S. Code § 351 outlines the conditions under which a drug or device is considered adulterated. If the parties only 'attempt to reasonably agree' on commercialization details, including manufacturing processes or quality control, there's a higher risk that the Assay could be produced in a way that doesn't meet required standards. This could lead to the introduction of an adulterated product into the market, directly contradicting the statute's goal of preventing the distribution of unsafe or ineffective drugs and devices. Defective commercialization agreements can indirectly lead to violations in the production, storage, and transport of product, leading to the product being considered adulterated. This is especially true if a lack of agreement leads to inadequate quality checks, poor ingredient controls, or improper manufacturing processes.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 21 U.S. Code § 351 - Adulterated drugs and devices | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 21 U.S. Code § 351 - Adulterated drugs and devices U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next A drug or device shall be deemed to be adulterated— (a) Poisonous, insanitary, etc., ingredients; adequate controls in manufacture (1) If it consists in whole or in part of any filthy, putrid, or decomposed substance; or (2)(A) if it has been prepared, packed, or held under insanitary conditions whereby it may have been contaminated with filth, or whereby it may have been rendered injurious to health; or (B) if it is a drug and the methods used in, or the facilities or controls used for, its manufacture, processing, packing, or holding do not conform to or are not operated or administered in conformity with current good manufacturing practice to assure that such drug meets the requirements of this chapter as to safety and has the identity and strength, and meets the quality and purity characteristics, which it purports or is represented to possess; or (C) if it is a compounded positron emission tomography drug and the methods used in, or the facilities and controls used for, its compounding, processing, packing, or holding do not conform to or are not operated or administered in conformity with the positron emission tomography compounding standards and the official monographs of the United States Pharmacopoeia to assure that such drug meets the requirements of this chapter as to safety and has the identity and strength, and meets the quality and purity characteristics, that it purports or is represented to possess; or (3) if its container is composed, in whole or in part, of any poisonous or deleterious substance which may render the contents injurious to health; or (4) if (A) it bears or contains, for purposes of coloring only, a color additive which is unsafe within the meaning of section 3
perturbation.justification: The change from 'will reasonably agree' to 'will attempt to reasonably agree' weakens the commitment to reaching an agreement on commercialization details. This introduces uncertainty, potentially leading to inadequate quality control and manufacturing processes. 21 U.S. Code § 351, as shown in both snippets, addresses adulterated drugs and devices, stating a drug or device shall be deemed adulterated if 'the methods used in, or the facilities or controls used for, its manufacture, processing, packing, or holding do not conform'. By only attempting to agree, the parties create a risk that the Assay could be produced without conforming to required manufacturing standards, directly contradicting the statute's goal of preventing the distribution of unsafe drugs.
